turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I also have the opinion for the Court today in No. 22-105, Coinbase v. Bielski. When a Federal District Court denies a motion to compel arbitration, the losing party has a statutory right to an immediate appeal. The sole question here is whether the District Court must stay its pre-trial and trial proceedings while that interlocutory appeal is ongoing. The answer is yes. The District Court must stay its proceedings. The case arises out of a putative class action filed against Coinbase in the District Court for the Northern District of California. Bielski sued on behalf of Coinbase users who alleged that Coinbase failed to replace funds fraudulently taken from their accounts. The Coinbase user agreement requires arbitration of disputes, but the District Court here denied Coinbase's motion to compel arbitration. Coinbase then filed an immediate appeal to the Ninth Circuit contending that the case belonged in arbitration rather than in Federal Court. Importantly for present purposes, Coinbase also moved to stay District Court proceedings pending resolution of the arbitrability issue on appeal. The District Court however declined to stay its proceedings and then Ninth Circuit likewise declined its stay. There's disagreement among the Courts of Appeals about whether a stay of District Court proceedings should be granted while the appeal of arbitrability is ongoing. So this Court granted review and now reversed the judgment of the Ninth Circuit. Under the relevant statute here when a District Court denies the parties' motion to compel arbitration that party may take an immediate appeal even though the case is not yet final in the District Court. That's a rare statutory exception to the usual rule that parties may not appeal before final judgment. The statute does not say however, is silent on whether the District Court proceedings must be stayed while the arbitrability issue is being decided on appeal. But Congress enacted this statute against a clear background principle in our view prescribed by this Court's precedents. An appeal including an interlocutory appeal divests the District Court of its control over those aspects of the case involved in the appeal. That principle procedure resolves this case. When a party appeals the denial of a motion to compel arbitration, the question of whether the litigation may go forward in the District Court is precisely what the Court of Appeals must decide meaning that the District Court proceeding should be stayed while the appeal is ongoing. Most Courts of Appeals to address the question have reached the same conclusion, leading treatises also agree. The common practice therefore in this situation is for a District Court to stay its proceedings while the appeal is ongoing. As we see it that common practice reflects common sense absent an automatic stay, Congress' decision to afford a right to an immediate appeal would be largely nullified. If the District Court could move forward while pre-trial and trial proceedings were ongoing, many of the asserted benefits of arbitration such as efficiency, less expense, less intrusive discovery and the like would be irretrievably lost even if the Court of Appeals later concluded that the case had actually belonged in arbitration all along. Absent a stay, parties also could be forced to settle to avoid the District Court proceedings that they contracted to avoid through arbitration. Put simply as we see it, it makes little sense for trial to go forward while the Court of Appeals cogitates on whether there should be a trial in the first place. In our view that a right to interlocutory appeal of the arbitrability issue without an automatic stay of the District Court proceedings would be like a lock without a key, a bat without a ball, a computer without a keyboard; in other words, as we see it not especially sensible. From the judiciaries' institutional perspective moreover allowing the case to proceed simultaneously in the District Court and the Court of Appeals creates the possibility that the District Court will weigh scarce judicial resources, which could be devoted to other pressing criminal or civil matters on a dispute that will ultimately head to arbitration in any event. In sum, we conclude that after Coinbase appealed from the denial of its motion to compel arbitration, the District Court was required to stay its proceedings. We reverse the judgment of the Ninth Circuit. Justice Jackson has filed a dissenting opinion, which Justices Sotomayor and Kagan have joined in full, in which Justice Thomas has joined in parts.
